pagine.testo: You wake up on a marble floor as cold as the bottom of a lake. The air smells of wax, old wood, and something metallic you can't quite place. Above you, a crystal chandelier sways slowly, though there's no wind. You sit up, your head pounding, and realize you're in the grand foyer of Villa Morvell—or so the rusty brass plaque on the nearest wall says. You don't remember how you got here. You don't even remember what it's like outside. There's only this villa, these ivory-colored stone walls, and two doors before you: one red, one black. Between the two doors, a grandfather clock beats slowly, like a tired heart. On the floor, next to your hand, is a folded piece of paper. You open it: "Choose carefully. The villa remembers every step." You put the paper in your pocket and get up.

scelte.testo: Open the RED door
Open the BLACK door

pagine.testo: The red door gives with a long, low groan, as if the villa were sighing. Beyond the threshold is a circular room lined with mirrors. In each mirror you see your reflection, but with a delay of a few seconds—and in at least one of them your reflection smiles at you when you're not smiling. You walk toward the center of the room. The floor is made of octagonal tiles painted with symbols you don't recognize. In the center, an alabaster pedestal supports a sphere of opaque glass. Inside the sphere something moves—a shadow, a breath, perhaps an unspoken word. You touch the sphere with a finger. A shiver runs up your arm to the back of your neck. Suddenly, all the lights go out. When they come back on, you're back in the hall. On the floor where you were before, there's now a footprint—but not your shoe. Someone—or something—was there with you.

scelte.testo: Catch your breath and return to the gates
Examine the footprint on the floor

pagine.testo: The black door has no handle. You push it with your palm and it swings inward, as silently as if the hinges had been oiled this morning. Beyond is a long, narrow corridor, lit by gas lights along the walls. The flames burn a soft blue, casting long shadows on the ceiling. As you walk, your shoes make no sound on the stone floor. The corridor curves slightly to the right, then again, and you find yourself in front of a stone staircase leading down and a wooden one leading up. From the stone one, a smell of earth and dampness rises. From the wooden one comes—and it takes several seconds before you recognize it—the scent of freshly brewed coffee. You hesitate on the edge of the first step.

scelte.testo: Go down the stone stairs
Climb up the wooden ladder

pagine.testo: You kneel beside the footprint. It's small—too small for an adult—and has six toes. The outline is perfect, as if someone had pressed a stamp of flesh into the marble. Around the footprint, you notice other details you hadn't seen before: a series of concentric circles etched into the floor, so fine they look like random scratches, but in reality perfectly regular. You're about to get up when the grandfather clock chimes three times. On the third strike, the floor trembles slightly, and you feel a mechanical click from beneath your feet. A floor panel lifts a centimeter. You find a secret compartment. Inside is an old book with a black leather cover, without a title. You open it at random. All the pages are blank except one, in the middle of the volume, on which is written in red ink: "The third window of the upper floor. Look at it when the moon is high. There's no moon outside. There's not even a window in the atrium. But the mansion may have upper floors you haven't explored yet.

scelte.testo: Looking for a staircase to the upper floor
Ignore the book and return to the doors

pagine.testo: The stone staircase descends many steps—you count thirty-seven before losing count—and then opens into a huge cellar. The walls are covered with wine bottles arranged in rusty iron racks. The ceiling is so low that you have to walk slightly bent. At the far end of the cellar, there is a dim light. You approach. The light comes from an oil lamp hanging from a hook. Beneath the lamp, sitting on a broken chair, is a wooden mannequin dressed as a butler. It has painted glass eyes, and on its jacket is a tag: "JEEVES. FAITHFUL SERVANT. DO NOT TOUCH." Despite the warning, you notice that the mannequin's right hand is clutching something: a golden key. As you move closer, the mannequin's head rotates toward you exactly forty-five degrees, with a slow, deliberate creak. You freeze. The cellar suddenly smells of burning.

scelte.testo: Take the key from the dummy's hand
Run up the stairs

pagine.testo: You look for a staircase in the hall but find none. You circle the grandfather clock, explore every corner, push the walls for secret passages. Nothing. Then you notice something strange: one of the wooden panels on the right wall has a grain that forms the outline of a door. There is no handle, no visible lock. You press where the center of the panel should be. Nothing. Press in the upper left corner. Nothing. Then you remember the book—"the third window"—and count in your head. The panel is the third from the left. Press in the upper right corner. With a satisfied click, the panel swings open toward you, revealing a spiral iron staircase. You climb up. The iron creaks under your feet with each step. You come to a small landing with three doors. All identical—dark wood, brass handle—except for a number engraved at the bottom: 1, 2, 3.

scelte.testo: Open door number 1
Open door number 2
Open door number 3

pagine.testo: The wooden staircase ascends two flights of stairs—the boards creak, one gives slightly under your left foot but holds—and emerges into a huge kitchen. Copper and brass everywhere: pots, pans, ladles, all hanging from hooks from the ceiling like a suspended army. On the large oak table in the center is a lit moka pot and a cup already prepared. The coffee is steaming. Someone was here a few minutes ago. You look around but there's no one. On the table, next to the cup, is a piece of paper handwritten in very thin handwriting:  If you're reading this, you've chosen well. The kitchen is the heart of the villa. Look for the drawer that won't open. You explore the drawers. There are dozens of them, lining every wall. Most contain cutlery, utensils, spices. But one—the fifth drawer in the bottom row under the sink—has a knob but won't open. Not at all. As if it were bricked up from the inside.

scelte.testo: Looking for something to pry open the drawer with
Drink coffee and explore beyond the kitchen

pagine.testo: You reach for the key, moving slowly, as if you were holding a dog you don't know. The mannequin's fingers seem to be gripping the key with a force impossible for wood. You pull. The mannequin's hand gives way, but the moment the key leaves its clawed fingers, the entire cellar is filled with a mechanical sound—a frantic ticking, like a thousand clocks all speeding up at once. The lights from all the wine bottles are reflected strangely. The mannequin turns its head completely toward you. The painted mouth smiles—but was it a smile before? You're not sure. You clutch the key and run. Up the stone stairs, through the corridor, into the foyer. Only in the foyer do you manage to stop and look at the key. It's gold, engraved with a pattern of intertwined snakes, and engraved on the code is one word: ABOVE. Maybe it opens something upstairs.

scelte.testo: Looking for a ladder to the upper floor
Return to the black corridor to explore

pagine.testo: Door number 1 opens onto a library. Not a small library—a library that seems impossible to exist in this space: shelves rising to a very high ceiling, wooden stairs sliding along brass tracks, and books. Thousands of books. All bound in leather, all without titles on the spine. You pick a volume at random. Open the first page. Inside is your story—this story, written in the third singular, with you as the protagonist. There are details you have not told anyone—thoughts, hesitations, even the way you stumbled on the second step of the ladder. You flip the pages. The story continues up to this precise moment:  ...he opened door number 1 and found the library. Then the pages are blank. Blank except for the last one, which has only an arrow pointing down.

scelte.testo: Follow the sign and go back downstairs
Continue exploring the library

pagine.testo: Door number 2 doesn't reveal a room. It reveals emptiness—or so it seems at first. Then your eyes adjust, and you understand: it's a completely dark room, and from the ceiling hang hundreds of mirrors of various shapes and sizes, all facing upward. In the floor in the center of the room is a circular hole from which a bright white light shines. You approach it carefully, touching the edge with the toe of your shoe. The hole is about a meter in diameter. You look inside: it's a well, and down perhaps ten meters there is water—water that glows with its own light. You notice that around the edge of the well are twelve letters engraved in random order. Some are bleached, others are stained with something dark. You're trying to decipher them when you hear a noise behind you—the door is closing.

scelte.testo: Try to block the door before it closes
Let the door close and study the well

pagine.testo: Door number 3. Beyond is an office—a massive mahogany desk, green leather chairs, a bookcase filled with binders and documents, an unlit fireplace with the ashes still warm. On the desk, under a green glass paperweight, is a document. You read it. It's a contract. A sales contract. The seller is "Ettore Morvell, last descendant." The buyer is you—or at least your name is there, written in perfect cursive that isn't your handwriting. You bought Villa Morvell. The date is three weeks ago. The price is: "the most precious memory you possess." At that moment, you understand why you don't remember anything from before the villa. You're reading the final clause—"the contractor accepts that the villa becomes his only reality"—when the fireplace lights itself. From the ashes, as if someone had flipped a switch, flames leap tall and blue.

scelte.testo: Looking for something among the ashes of the fireplace
Rummaging through the desk for something else

pagine.testo: You look for something to pry open the drawer with. In a wall cabinet, you find a large, heavy kitchen knife. You slide the blade into the crevice of the drawer and pry. The wood creaks, protests, bends—and then the drawer pops open. There's nothing inside. Absolutely nothing. Not a nail, not a speck of dust, not a cobweb. The bottom of the drawer is perfectly clean and a surgical white that clashes with everything else in the kitchen. Then you realize that the bottom isn't wood. It's paper. You press with a finger and the paper gives way, revealing a cavity. Inside the cavity is an envelope sealed with red sealing wax in the shape of an eye. On the front of the envelope, written in the same paper-thin handwriting as the paper on the table: "Open only when you're ready to know." You're not sure you're ready. But you open it anyway.

scelte.testo: Read the contents of the envelope
Put the envelope back in the drawer and move on

pagine.testo: You walk into the library. Each book you take out tells your story up until the moment you opened it—then blank pages. It's as if the villa were writing in real time, using you as ink. You find books that tell of lives you might have lived. In one, you're a sailor. In another, a painter. In a third, an ordinary, happy person who has never entered a cursed villa. You hold that book a little tighter before putting it back. At the back of the library is a ladder that leads to a mezzanine. On the mezzanine is a single lectern, and on the lectern, a single book—larger than the others, bound in something you'd rather not identify. The title on the spine, the only title visible in the entire library, reads: "HOW TO EXIT VILLA MORVELL." Below, in smaller type: "First edition. Only one copy. As you climb the stairs, a step breaks under your foot and you fall, fortunately without any serious injuries. When you get up, you're back downstairs, in the hall.

scelte.testo: Return to the atrium doors
Look for the stairs to the upper floor

pagine.testo: You drink the coffee. It's perfect—just the right temperature, just the right amount of bitterness, with a cardamom aftertaste you weren't expecting. For a moment, you feel almost normal, almost human, almost out of this whole thing. Then you notice a door on the opposite side of the kitchen, half open. You push it. Beyond is a pantry—shelves of jars, sacks of flour, stacks of bottles—and at the back, partially hidden by a sack of rice, a trapdoor in the floor. The trapdoor has a rusty iron ring instead of a handle. You lift it. It makes a long, melancholy creak. Below is a ladder, and below the ladder is a light—not the dim light of the wine cellar, but a warm, orange light, like a live fire. You go down. The ladder leads to a narrow tunnel, lined with red brick, which ends after about thirty meters in a rough wooden door with a small grated window.

scelte.testo: Spy through the latticed window before opening
Open the door without looking

pagine.testo: The door slams shut. You're in complete darkness, with only the light from the well. You kneel at the edge and stare into the luminous water. The letters carved around the edge, you can see better now: V, I, T, A, M, O, R, T, E, L, U, X. You're trying to piece them together into something sensible when the water in the well begins to move. It rises. Slowly but surely, the luminous water rises up the walls of the well. In a few minutes it will be at the edge of the floor. In a few minutes after that it will be at your ankle. You search desperately for a way to open the door. There's no handle from the inside. You push, you bang, you scream. The door doesn't budge. The water touches the soles of your shoes—it's warm, almost pleasantly so, and it's no ordinary water: where it hits the floor it leaves a pattern of light like phosphorus. Then the water stops. Right at the edge of the well, The water settles. And in the bright reflection of the water on the mirrored ceiling, a word appears for a second: ABOVE.

scelte.testo: Look into the well again
Looking for something on the mirrored ceiling

pagine.testo: You approach the fireplace with the blue flames. The heat is real but it doesn't burn—or at least, not in the way you'd expect. You put your hand in and the flames envelop it without consuming it. You rummage through the hot ashes. You find something hard. You pull it out: it's a dark metal medallion, round, on a chain. On the front is engraved a villa—Villa Morvell, unmistakable, seen from above. On the back is a date: today's date. How can it be today's date? How do you know what today's date is, when you don't remember anything else? Suddenly you remember. Just for an instant—a flash—you see yourself outside the villa, on a normal afternoon, with a newspaper in your hand. The date on the newspaper. The date on the medallion. Identical. The memory fades. You're back in the office, with the medallion in your hand and the blue flames slowly turning yellow, then orange, then dying out altogether. The fireplace is once again full of cold ash.

scelte.testo: Keep the medallion and return to the corridor
Replace the medallion in the ashes and examine the room

pagine.testo: You rummage in the desk. First drawer: pens, stamps, red sealing wax. Second drawer: letters—dozens of letters, all addressed to "Ettore Morvell", all open, all written in different languages ​​you don't recognize except one: the last one, in Italian, in very thin handwriting that's starting to sound familiar. "Dear Ettore, the mechanism is ready. They will always find the red door before the black one—it's in the nature of men to choose visible danger. But you've built the paths well. Only coffee will carry them forward. Only coffee. Don't forget: the kitchen is the heart. — V. Third drawer: a loaded revolver. You don't know how to shoot, but you put it in your pocket anyway. Fourth drawer, the lowest: empty, except that at the bottom is written in pencil, in a hasty, nervous handwriting completely different from the others: "IF YOU FIND HER, RUN THROUGH THE TUNNEL. DON'T STOP. DON'T LOOK BACK. The fifth drawer is locked.

scelte.testo: Use something to open the fifth drawer
Take the letter and return to the corridor

pagine.testo: Inside the envelope is a single sheet of paper. Written on both sides, tightly packed, in microscopic characters. You bring it to the window to read it better—but the kitchen has no windows. You bring it under the light of the moka pot, which strangely casts more light than usual. The letter is the story of Villa Morvell. A villa built in 1847 by an architect whose name is crossed out. A villa designed as a labyrinth—not to keep people out, but to keep them in. "Each guest," the letter says, "feeds the villa with their own confusion. The more they get lost, the more the villa grows. I counted sixty-two guests before me. I didn't count myself." Then, at the end, in slightly larger letters: "There is only one way out. You don't go out through the doors. You don't go out through the windows. You get out when you stop trying to get out, and instead find the heart of the villa — and turn it off. Then: "The heart beats in the tunnel. Put the letter back in the envelope.

scelte.testo: Look for the tunnel mentioned in the letter
Ignore the instructions and explore the kitchen further

pagine.testo: You swing the door open. Beyond is a circular room with a fireplace in the center—an open-air fireplace, with a very high flame that burns without consuming wood, because there is no wood. Just fire on bare stone. Around the fireplace, arranged on stone pedestals, are six masks. Carnival masks—some beautiful, some grotesque. Each has a number carved into its forehead. You walk around the fireplace, studying them. Then you notice that one mask is missing. Pedestal number 4 is empty, but it still bears the marks where the mask once rested. And on the empty pedestal is carved an arrow pointing downward. You feel the heat of the fire on your face. It's pleasant—too pleasant. Your eyelids begin to feel heavy. The heat of the flame without fuel has something hypnotic about it.

scelte.testo: Resist hypnosis and return to the tunnel
Giving in to sleep in front of the fire

pagine.testo: You look into the well again. The bright water reflects your face—but it also reflects something behind you. You whip around: there's nothing there. You look back at the reflection. In the water, clearly, there's a figure standing behind you. Tall, thin, with something shining in its hand. You turn again. Nothing. You look at the reflection. The figure has taken a step toward you. This time you don't turn around. You watch the reflection as the figure gets closer and closer. When it's very close—close enough to touch you—you see what it's holding: a key. The same golden key with entwined snakes that might be in the wine cellar. The figure's reflection reaches out toward your reflection. In the reflection, your reflection takes the key. Then you feel something cold in your real hand. You make a fist. When you open it, you have the key.

scelte.testo: Go find out what this key opens
Throw the key into the well

pagine.testo: You look at the mirrored ceiling. The word has disappeared, but the thousand reflected fragments of your face all stare back at you from different angles. Then you notice one that doesn't look like you—a reflection with different hair, a scar on your cheek that you don't have, a smile you're not smiling. The reflection beckons you with a finger. It points downward. Then it disappears, leaving only your normal face reflected in its place. You look where it was pointing. On the floor, at that precise spot, one of the tiles is slightly raised. You lift it. Beneath it is a button—a simple, round wooden button, like those on old doorbells. Press it. With a mechanical whir, the door you thought was bricked up reopens. You exit the well room and find yourself back on the landing with the three doors. But now there is a fourth door, which wasn't there before, with a single word engraved above it: UNDER.

scelte.testo: Open the door with the word BELOW written on it
Ignore the new door and return to the lobby

pagine.testo: You peek through the grate. Beyond the door is a low, long room—like a crypt, but instead of burial vaults there are rough wooden shelves, and on the shelves are objects: clocks, compasses, microscopes, sextants, instruments you don't recognize. In the center of the room is a stone table on which lies open a huge book, the pages held open by two stones. And sitting at the table, with her back to you, is a person. A real person. Or at least, she seems real—she's breathing, moving slowly, turning the pages of the huge book with long, thin fingers. You exhale and open the door as quietly as possible. The hinge creaks. The person stops. She turns slowly. She's an old woman—very old, with light eyes and white hair tied back—and she doesn't seem surprised to see you. In fact, she looks like she's been waiting for you. "You're slower than the others," she says. "It usually takes three hours. It took you..." he checks a pocket watch, "...much longer. Sit down." He hesitates in the doorway.

scelte.testo: Come in and sit down
Ask who it is before approaching

pagine.testo: "Who are you?" you ask. Your voice sounds strange in the dry air of the crypt—a little hoarse, as if you haven't spoken in hours. Maybe you haven't spoken in hours. The woman raises her hands—a gesture of peaceful surrender—and smiles. The smile is genuine, but there's something tired behind it, like someone who's explained the same thing countless times and already knows how it's going to end. "I'm the caretaker," she says. "Hector left me here when he sold the villa. Or maybe I was here before him—I don't even remember at this point. The villa has this effect on time." She points to the chair in front of her. "Sit down. If you don't, the villa will send you back." As if to confirm her words, the tunnel behind you emits a sound—a kind of sucking, a mechanical call. You feel your feet shift back a step, even though you didn't move them intentionally.

scelte.testo: Resist and sit by the keeper
Letting yourself be dragged by the tunnel

pagine.testo: You return to the hall for the third time. Or the fourth. You've lost count. The grandfather clock looks at you—or so it seems—with something that might be derision. There's something new on the floor: a letter. It wasn't there before. You pick it up. The envelope is white, your name written on it in characters you don't recognize. Inside, a note with three words: "Drink coffee." You didn't remember the coffee. Now you remember it. The black door is still there, silent and patient.

scelte.testo: Going through the black door again
Sit in the lobby and wait for something to happen

pagine.testo: You sit on the cold marble floor, back against the wall, and wait. The grandfather clock swings. The candle flames (when did candles appear? They weren't there before) flicker. The ceiling of the room is very high, and in the darkness above you, you can't see anything. You wait five minutes. Ten. Twenty. Then the grandfather clock stops. The pendulum stops swinging halfway, suspended in the air like a forgotten weight. In the absolute silence that follows, you hear—far away, through the walls, as if coming from the very foundations of the villa—a voice. Not words. A sound. A long, regular tone. The pendulum starts again. The clock shows a different time than before. You've been sitting here for five minutes. The clock has lost—or gained—three hours. "Time doesn't work here," says a voice nearby. You turn sharply. There's no one there. But next to you, where a second ago there was only floor, there is a steaming cup of coffee.

scelte.testo: Drinking coffee
Leave the cafe and return to the doors

pagine.testo: You walk around the perimeter of the keeper's room, touching objects on the shelves. Each object you touch makes a different sound—not the sound you'd expect, but something deeper, as if each object contains a fragment of a forgotten conversation. A sextant: the sound of wind on a sea that doesn't exist. A wristwatch: the sound of a muffled cry. A compass: the sound of a door closing. A strange instrument with multiple lenses you can't identify: no sound—absolute silence, as if it had absorbed all the noises. The keeper watches you do this without interrupting. Then: "What you're looking for isn't in these objects. These are remnants—things left behind by others. You need to move on." "Move on where?" She points to the book on the table.

scelte.testo: Read the book on the table
Sit back and listen to the caretaker

pagine.testo: The book is a plan. Not a normal architectural drawing, but something more alive—the lines seem to move slightly, the corridors change shape as you look at them, as if you were watching a breathing organism. The caretaker approaches and points to a spot on the plan. "You are here," she says. "And there," she moves her finger, "is the heart." The spot she points to is at the geometric center of the entire plan, marked with a small red spiral. "How do I get there?" "There is only one path. All the others take you back to the beginning. The path goes through this room, then the right corridor, then the gear room, then down, then up. It's not complicated if you know where to go." "And if I don't know where to go?" "Then the villa will teach you where not to go, until all you have left is the right path." She shrugs. "It's its favorite teaching method."

scelte.testo: Ask for the right corridor
Ask for the gear room

pagine.testo: The corridor on the right," the caretaker explains, "starts at the back of this room. It looks like a dead end, but it isn't—you must keep your left hand touching the left wall at all times. Never stop. If you stop, the corridor changes and you'll get lost." "For how long?" "It depends. Some people have taken years." Said with complete normalcy, as if commenting on the traffic. "But if you maintain contact with the left wall, you'll reach the gear room in a few minutes." You stand, ready to go. The caretaker holds you back with a hand on your arm—her grip is surprisingly firm for such an old hand. "One thing," she says. "In the gear room, you'll find a choice. It will seem obvious. Do the opposite of what seems obvious to you." Before you can ask her to elaborate, she gestures toward the back of the room. There is indeed a corridor you thought was just a shadow.

scelte.testo: Follow the corridor keeping your left hand on the wall
Return to the caretaker with more questions

pagine.testo: "The gear room," says the caretaker, and for the first time in the conversation, something changes in her tone—not fear, exactly, but cautious respect. "It's the mechanism of the mansion. All those corridors that change, all the doors that appear and disappear, all those clocks—they come from there." "So if you turn off the gears..." "The mansion stops. Yes." Pause. "But the gears can't be turned off just like that. There's a precise order. If you get the order wrong, the gears speed up instead of slowing down, and then..." she pauses. "And then?" "And then the mansion goes to a place where you can't follow it. And you stay." Silence. "But the correct order?" "I don't know. No caretaker has ever known that. You find it inside." He points down the corridor. "Go. And remember what I told you before: do the opposite of what seems obvious to you.

scelte.testo: Go to the right corridor
Thank the keeper and ask again

pagine.testo: You enter the corridor. It's narrow—your shoulders almost touch the walls on either side. You immediately place your left hand on the left wall and begin walking. The wall is cold, damp stone, like the wall of a cave. The corridor turns. Again. Again. You go up a few steps, down a few steps. Every now and then the light changes—from orange to white to green to almost nothing. But your left hand never leaves the wall. At a certain point, the corridor becomes so narrow that you have to walk sideways. Then it widens again. Then the floor rises at a steep angle. Your left hand doesn't leave the wall. Your left hand is the only real thing in this ever-changing corridor. Then, suddenly, the left wall ends. You find yourself standing on the edge of a huge space. You've found it. The gear room.

scelte.testo: Enter the gear room
Stop at the threshold and observe before entering

pagine.testo: You pause in the doorway and stare. The room is enormous—as tall as a cathedral, as wide as a town square. And filled with gears. Gears of every size, from the smallest (as big as a fingernail) to the largest (as tall as a three-story building, a dark iron cogwheel turning so slowly you could almost miss it). The gears are connected to each other in an incomprehensible three-dimensional network. Some turn clockwise, some counterclockwise. Some are iron, some brass, some something translucent you don't recognize. They all emit a constant hum—not annoying, almost musical—that you feel in your bones more than in your ears. In the center of the room is something you can't see clearly from here. A structure, perhaps. An altar? A machine within a machine? Two paths lead to the center: one direct, across an iron walkway suspended between the gears. The other winding, following a path on the floor between the bases. of the larger gears.

scelte.testo: Take the elevated walkway
Follow the path on the floor

pagine.testo: You step onto the catwalk. From above, the room is even more impressive—you can see how all the gears connect, how the movement of one transfers energy to dozens of others in a perfect mechanical cascade. It's almost beautiful. Then the catwalk begins to shake. A gear the size of a bus passes just inches from your head—it hadn't seen you before, or perhaps the catwalk has shifted. You remain still until it passes. Then another. Then another. You're halfway across the catwalk when a gear hits the structure's main support. The catwalk buckles, a bolt gives way, and before you can do anything, you're flying down. A four-meter fall, landing on the stone floor—painful but not fatal. When you stand up, you're on the floor's path, near the center. Your body has made up its mind for you.

scelte.testo: Continue towards the center of the room
Go back to the corridor

pagine.testo: You sit down. The caretaker sits across from you. For a long moment, no one speaks. Then she opens a drawer in the table and takes out two porcelain mugs and a thermos from which she pours something dark and steaming. It's not coffee—it tastes of herbs and burnt wood, bitter but not unpleasant. "The others," she says finally, "usually come here with more questions." "I have a lot of questions." "I know. But questions in the villa are dangerous. Every question is a fork in the road, and every fork in the road is an opportunity to get lost." He drinks from his cup. "Answers are better." "Then tell me how to get out." She nods, as if that were the right answer. "You'll find the gear room. In the center of the room is the heart of the mansion. It stops with a key—but not just any key. The right key is the one you found, or will find, or was given to you. Do you have a key?

scelte.testo: Show the golden key with the snakes (if you have it)
Admit that you have no key

pagine.testo: "I don't have any keys." The caretaker nods slowly. "Then you must find one. There are three in the house. One is in the cellar, in Jeeves's possession. One is in the hall of mirrors, at the bottom of the well—you must get it from someone. And one..." she hesitates. "And one?" "I have one." From her apron pocket, she pulls a golden key, identical to the one you saw—or will see—in the mannequin's hands. The entwined snakes glint in the dim light of the crypt. "I have something to give you in return." "What?" "A memory. A real one. From before the villa." You think about it. Do you have memories? Yes—one, recovered in front of the blue fireplace: yourself with a newspaper, outside the villa, on a normal afternoon. You share it with her, in words. She listens with her eyes closed. Then she nods and hands you the key. "Go to the gear room. Do you know the way?

scelte.testo: Ask for the way to the gear room
Say that you already know the way

pagine.testo: You take out the key. The caretaker examines it—turns it in her fingers, holds it up to the light, reads the word engraved on the code. "Good," she says simply. As if she already knew. "This is the right one. Do you know how to use it yet?" "No." "In the center of the gear room is a central mechanism. It looks like a clock, but it isn't. It has a lock—not immediately visible, you have to look for it on the left side, under the third wheel. Insert the key. Turn it three times counterclockwise, then once clockwise. Not the other way around—many do the opposite." "And then?" "And then the villa stops. And you can leave." "Is it that simple?" He grimaces. "Seven people have figured it out, in all these years. Simple, it depends." He stands up and picks up the cups. "The right corridor leads to the gear room. Keep your left hand on the left wall the entire way." He turns to a shelf and begins arranging items as if you were already gone.

scelte.testo: Follow the instructions and go to the corridor on the right
Ask the caretaker one last question

pagine.testo: "One last thing," you say. The caretaker stops without turning. "Ettore Morvell. Who was he?" A long silence. Then: "He was someone like you. Entered the villa by mistake—or so he thought. Stayed to understand. Stayed too long. The villa absorbed him." "Absorbed how?" She finally turns. "The villa feeds on the people who stay there. It doesn't kill them. It integrates them. They become part of the architecture. They become corridors, rooms, mechanisms." Pause. "Ettore is that stone staircase that goes down to the cellar. Every time someone walks down it, it's him." You don't know what to say. The caretaker adds, much more quietly: "I've been here since before you arrived. I'll be here after. But you can still leave. Don't waste the opportunity asking questions." She points to the corridor.

scelte.testo: Go to the right corridor
Ask what other absorbed people have become

pagine.testo: "The others? Those who stayed?" The caretaker sighs. She sits down again. She understands that you won't leave until you have this answer. "The mannequin in the cellar—Jeeves—was the royal butler of the mansion. He stayed voluntarily. He thought it was his duty to protect the key even afterward." Pause. "The woman in the mirrored well came here fifty years ago. She was a researcher. She thought she could study the mansion from the inside. Now she is the reflection." "And you? What have you become?" "I am memory." Simply put. "Every mansion needs something to remind it of itself from the inside. Otherwise, the mansion forgets itself and collapses." He raises a hand. "Don't ask me if I'm happy. That's not an applicable category." Silence. Then: "Now go. If you stay here and talk to me, the villa will conclude that you integrated voluntarily and you'll start to become something. Probably that missing window in the foyer.

scelte.testo: Go immediately to the right corridor
Return to the hall to reflect

pagine.testo: The Jeeves mannequin still has its head turned toward you. Its painted expression is neutral—neither hostile nor benevolent—but that forty-five-degree turn of the head gives the whole thing an air of unsettling attention. You sit on the floor in front of him. You've decided to study him before taking the key. After a while, you notice that the key in his hand isn't just resting—it's wrapped around the wooden fingers with too much precision to be casual. As if he's offering it. Not holding. "Are you offering it?" You ask aloud, not expecting a response. The mannequin's head rotates another forty-five degrees—it's now looking in a different direction. And the hand opens slightly. The key is now clearly offered, not held. You take it without resistance. On the mannequin's wooden palm, where the key was, is carved a tiny word that wasn't there before—or that you hadn't seen: WAIT.

scelte.testo: Wait in the cellar to see what happens
Take the key and go back up

pagine.testo: Wait. The cellar is silent except for the distant dripping of water. The Jeeves mannequin no longer moves. The wine bottles look like eyes in the darkness. After a few minutes—or a few hours, it's impossible to tell—a bottle falls by itself from a shelf and shatters on the floor. From the dark liquid that spills across the floor, a rolled-up sheet of paper tied with string emerges, floating. You open it. It's a map—rough, hand-drawn, but clearly of the mansion. There are annotations in a handwriting you recognize as that of the note you found at the beginning. The path to "the heart" is marked with a red line. The line starts in the kitchen. At the bottom of the map, a note: "Do not enter through the door of the mask room. Do not yield to fire. Coffee is the heart of everything." You fold the map and put it in your pocket along with the note. Now you have two maps and a key. This is starting to make a respectable inventory.

scelte.testo: Go back up and go to the kitchen
Exploring the cellar further

pagine.testo: You continue exploring the cellar. Among the furthest racks you find a hidden passage—a rotting wooden door that almost falls off when you push it. Beyond is another, smaller cellar, with a single shelf and a single bottle. The bottle is made of dark blue glass and has no label. Next to the bottle is a goblet. You pour a fingerful of the liquid into it. It's not wine—it's too dark, almost black, and has no smell. You hold it in your hand for a long moment. Then you throw it away without drinking. You don't drink black stuff from unlabeled bottles in cursed houses. That's a rule of life. On the back wall of the cellar is an inscription carved into the brick: "HE WHO DRINKS SEES. HE WHO SEES REMAINS. HE WHO REMAINS BECOMES." You quickly exit the cellar and return to the main cellar.

scelte.testo: Go back up the stone stairs
Go to Jeeves to get the key

pagine.testo: You return to the hall of mirrors. This time you know what to expect—or so you think. The lights are still there, the well is still in the center, the figure in the reflections is probably still there. But when you enter, the room has changed. The well is still in the center, but the water level is much higher—almost flush with the floor. And the mirrors on the ceiling no longer reflect your image. They reflect scenes—images of other places, like windows into parallel worlds. In one mirror, you see the keeper in her crypt, looking at you as if she were looking through the mirror. She beckons you. In her hand, she's holding something up to you—a piece of paper with something written on it that's too small to read from this distance. The water from the well touches your shoes.

scelte.testo: Try to get closer to the mirror to read the paper
Exit the room quickly

pagine.testo: You approach the specific mirror where the caretaker is watching you. The water is now ankle-deep—warm, bright, indifferent. The mirror is high, on the ceiling, so you have to look up. The caretaker moves the paper closer to the mirror's surface. You can read—with difficulty, some letters backwards—the words: "NOT THE CATWALK. THE FLOOR." Then the water suddenly rises to your knees, and the caretaker in the mirror shakes her head urgently. Pointing down—the floor beneath you. You look down. The bright water now surrounding your legs reveals something in the floor you hadn't seen: a plaque, carved into the bricks, with the same red spiral you saw on the plan of the villa in the crypt. The heart. It's not in the gear room. Or at least—not only there.

scelte.testo: Try to lift the license plate
Ignore the license plate and get out before the water rises

pagine.testo: You put your fingers under the edge of the plaque and pull. It's heavy—too heavy for you alone. The water rises again: now up to your waist. You have less time than you thought. You try with all your strength. The plaque moves—an inch, two—then falls back. The water is up to your chest. If it rises any further to the mouth of the well, which is now at floor level, you'll be swept away. Then you feel something unexpected: someone else is pulling the plaque from the other side. From the inside. With much more force than yours. The plaque jerks up, and the water begins to flow down, into the hole—as if it had found a drain. You look into the hole. There's a hand—the hand of the figure you see in the reflections—holding out something to you. A golden key with entwined snakes. Then the plaque closes by itself, the water is gone, and you're standing in the dry room with the key in your hand and the heart beating very fast.

scelte.testo: Go to the gear room with the key
Return to the caretaker to report

pagine.testo: The gear room greets you with its constant hum. Up close, the gears are even more impressive: you can see the texture of the metal, the striations of time, the rust stains that form almost decorative patterns. Some gears are so large that their upper edges are lost in the darkness above. Some are so small that you only hear them as a subtle tinkling. You walk toward the center, keeping an eye on the moving mechanisms. You have to pass under a gear that swings like a pendulum—you wait for the right moment and cross. You step over a metal chain as thick as your wrist that drags across the floor to who knows where. The center of the room. There it is. It's not an altar. It's not a clock. It's a column—cylindrical, perhaps three meters high, made of dark metal—from which all the other mechanisms emanate. Like a tree on which the entire forest depends. Around the base of the column, sitting on a circular step, is a sleeping man.

scelte.testo: Come closer to the sleeping man
Ignore it and look for the lock on the column

pagine.testo: You approach the man. He's old—older than the caretaker—with a long white beard and his hands clasped in his lap. He's breathing evenly. He's truly asleep. On his shabby jacket he has a tag, like Jeeves: "ETTORE MORVELL. OWNER. DO NOT DISTURB." It's not the staircase. It's not embedded in the architecture. It's a person—who's been asleep for who knows how long—next to the central column of his own villa. You wake him with a hand on his shoulder. It takes a while. Then he opens his eyes—they're clear and distant, like someone coming from a very deep dream. "Ah," he says. "You've arrived." No surprise. Just that vast tiredness. He struggles to his feet. "I've been waiting for someone to get here. It usually takes so long." He looks at you. "Do you have the key?

scelte.testo: Show him the golden key
Ask him who he is before showing the key

pagine.testo: "Who are you?" you ask, even though the card has already answered. "I'm Ettore Morvell," he says, as if confirming something already known. "I built this villa. Or at least, I thought I built it. In reality, the villa built itself, using my plans as guidance." He looks at his hands. "That's an important distinction." "The caretaker says you've become a ladder." A short, dry laugh. "The caretaker says many things that have been true at different times. I've been here—at this column—ever since I realized I couldn't stop it from the inside. I was hoping someone would come from the outside." "And now?" "Now you can stop it. With the key. If you have it." He looks at you with those distant eyes. "Do you have it?

scelte.testo: Show the golden key
Admitting you don't have the key

pagine.testo: "I don't have the key." Ettore closes his eyes for a moment. "Then you have to go find her. There are three in the villa. The caretaker has probably already told you." Pause. "Or she will—time is complicated here." "How do I get back here after I find her?" "Hold your left hand on the left wall in the corridor. It's the only way to get there directly. Otherwise, the villa will carry you around for hours." He sits back down on the step with the slowness of someone who hasn't been in a hurry for decades. "I'll come back and wait." "How long will you wait?" He opens his eyes. "As long as it takes." He closes his eyes again. In a few seconds, he seems asleep again. Exit the gear room the way you came in.

scelte.testo: Go get the key from the caretaker
Go get the key from the mannequin in the cellar

pagine.testo: You explore the edges of the gear room before approaching the center. Along the left wall, you find a series of dials—like pressure gauges on a boiler—indicating values ​​you don't understand. One of them has a needle in the red. Along the right wall, something different: a series of framed photographs. Not ordinary photographs—photographs of people, each at a different moment in the mansion. You recognize some of the places: the foyer, the library, the cellar. The people in the photographs seem alive—or at least, some of them. In one photograph, in the center, you recognize yourself in the foyer, at the precise moment you woke up. You're looking at your photograph when you realize it's moving. Slowly—you in the photo are rising from the floor, looking around, approaching the doors. It's a recording. The mansion is recording you in real time. You are in the photo even now—small, distant, looking at your own photo.

scelte.testo: Go to the center of the room
Look for a photograph that shows the way out

pagine.testo: You search through the photographs. Some show people you don't recognize in places you haven't yet explored. One shows the caretaker—much younger, smiling—in a garden you can't place in the villa. Then you find what you were looking for: a photograph of the center of the gear room, with the column. And in this photograph, you can clearly see, on the left side of the column, a lock. Small, octagonal, beneath a large cogwheel. You're memorizing the position when the photograph changes—the column in the photo begins to rotate slowly, showing different angles, like an instruction manual. It stops at the angle showing the lock, and stays still. As if it were saying: look carefully. You look carefully. Then you walk toward the center.

scelte.testo: Go to the center of the room with the instructions memorized
Return to the keeper to report the discovery

pagine.testo: Lost in the corridors of the mansion. You stopped holding your left hand on the wall for just a second—a moment of distraction, a second's distraction—and now you don't know where you are. The corridor you're in is unlike any you've walked before. The walls are a very dark brick red, the ceiling so low you have to walk hunched over, and from the floor grow—grow is the right word—stalactites of white salt that creak under your footsteps. It feels like an intestine. You don't want to think about it. There's only one way forward and only one way back. Forward is a curve. Back is the darkness from which you came.

scelte.testo: Move forward towards the curve
Going back into the darkness

pagine.testo: After the bend, the red corridor opens into a small vaulted room. In the center is a fountain—a real fountain, made of marble, with flowing water. The water is clear and makes the most normal sound you've ever heard in this villa: the sound of falling water. Next to the fountain is a bench. On the bench is a hat. Inside the hat is a note:  "The water is real. You can drink." Drink. The water is fresh and tastes like water—nothing else. No strange aftertaste, no hypnotic effect. Just water. You sit on the bench for a moment. Then you notice that the walls of the room are covered in writing. Dozens of names, dates, messages—like those left in prisons, on the walls.  Marco passed through here, day 47. Don't give in to the fire.  Coffee. Always the left hand.  V. knows the truth. Your name is there too. But you didn't write it.

scelte.testo: Trying to understand how your name is there
Ignore the writings and find a way out of the room

pagine.testo: Your name is written low down, near the floor, in a handwriting that looks like yours but isn't yours—it's like a mirror copy, or a copy made by someone who saw you write but never learned from you. Below your name, today's date. Below the date, a note: "He made it. He's the seventh." The seventh. The caretaker said seven had reached the heart. But you're still halfway there. Unless... Unless someone already knows how this ends. Next to your name is another name you recognize—Ettore Morvell—with the date many decades ago. And below his name: "He didn't make it. He stayed." The difference between you is subtle. He built the villa. You entered by chance. Maybe that's what matters.

scelte.testo: Get up and look for the exit from the room
Write something next to your name

pagine.testo: You're looking for an exit from the fountain room. Beyond the fountain is an archway that leads to a corridor—but this corridor is different from the red one you came from. It's higher, lighter, with walls of ordinary gray stone. You follow it. It curves once. Then again. Then it opens—suddenly, without warning—into the gear room. You entered from the opposite side from before. The central column is there, about thirty meters away. Ettore Morvell is still asleep on his step. The gears are whirring. Time in the villa flows as it will. But you still have the key in your pocket—or maybe not, you should check—and the lock is on the left side of the column, under the third wheel.

scelte.testo: Please check that you still have the key and go to the column
Wake Ettore Morvell before proceeding

pagine.testo: You try to open the lock without the key. With a pin you find in your pocket. With a piece of broken metal you retrieve from the floor. With brute force. With patience. Nothing works. The lock is octagonal and requires something specific. Not just anything. Ettore Morvell, still on his step, without opening his eyes: "You need the key." "I know." "Then go get it." Silence. The whirring of gears. "Were you asleep the whole time I was trying?" "I'm sleeping lightly." Pause. "The key is in the villa. There are three. One with the caretaker, one with the mannequin in the cellar, one in the mirrored well. You already have one and don't know it." You check your pockets.

scelte.testo: Find the key in your pocket (you came back with it)
Go and get the key from the caretaker

pagine.testo: You have the key. You have the instructions. You have the courage—or at least, something close to courage. You walk toward the central column. Ettore looks at you with those distant eyes. He doesn't say anything. His gaze, however, is different from before—there's something very close to hope, hidden beneath all that tiredness. The column is even more imposing up close. The dark metal radiates heat, and up close you can feel a subtle vibration, like a heartbeat. The heart of the villa. It really beats. On the left side, under the third cogwheel (count: one, two, three—this one) is the lock. Octagonal, like in the photographs. Insert the key. It fits perfectly—obviously, it was made for this. Pause. Three turns counterclockwise. One clockwise. Not the other way around. Take a breath.

scelte.testo: Turn the key three times counterclockwise, then once clockwise
Turn the key once clockwise, then three times counterclockwise (the opposite)

pagine.testo: You turn the key the wrong way—or at least, not the way you were told. The gears accelerate. A whirring that becomes a noise, then a crash. The vibration in the column increases until it becomes a tremor. Then an earthquake. Then something you have no words to describe. All the objects in the room begin to move. The large gears oscillate on their axis. The photographs on the walls fall. The floor undulates as if it were water. Ettore Morvell, on his bench, opens his eyes and says in the flat voice of someone who is not surprised: "You did the opposite." "I know. How does it stop?" "Turn again—now three counterclockwise, one clockwise. Fast." The acceleration of the gears makes everything more difficult—the column vibrates so much that holding onto it is difficult. But the key is still in the lock.

scelte.testo: Correct your aim: three counterclockwise, one clockwise
Take the key away and run away

pagine.testo: You grit your teeth and turn. The tremor makes it almost impossible to maintain a grip, but you turn—one, two, three counterclockwise—then one clockwise. The gears slow. Slowly. Resistantly. As if they don't want to stop. But they slow. The hum subsides. The tremor lessens. The photographs on the floor stop scrolling. Ettore Morvell closes his eyes with an expression that might be relief. Then the mechanism stops. Not suddenly—with a long, almost elegant deceleration, like the end of a piece of music. The last gear stops on a specific tooth, as if it was always meant to stop there. Silence. A true, complete silence, the first you've heard since you opened your eyes in this place. Then, somewhere in the villa—far away, through the walls—you hear the sound of a door opening.

scelte.testo: Follow the sound of the door
Standing by and watching the gears stop turning

pagine.testo: You take the key out and run. You don't know where you're going—the tremor has shifted the gears, the floor is slippery, the room feels different. You run toward what you remember as the exit. You can't find the exit. You circle the gears. Every time you think you've found the corridor, it ends in a wall. The gears accelerate again. The noise is deafening. Then a hand grabs your shoulder. It's Ettore Morvell, standing for the first time, with surprising energy considering how old he looked. "Don't run," he says loudly so you can hear him. "The key. Put it back. Three counterclockwise. One clockwise. Or the villa won't stop." "What if I put it back in?" "It still works. The villa won't take it away from you until you're done." You return to the column. The key trembles in your hand.

scelte.testo: Replace the key and correct the movement
Give in to panic and scream into the void

pagine.testo: You stand there looking at the still gears. It's the first time you've seen them still, and they're... different. Without the movement, without the vibration, the hum you perceived as constant and inevitable—without all of this, the room seems larger. And quieter. Then you realize that the gears aren't just mechanisms. Now that they're still, you can see inscriptions carved into the edges of many of them. Texts, phrases, names. As if the villa had written its own history on its walls. Read the closest one: "Here lies a second of September 1923, when Ettore Morvell realized he couldn't leave." The one next to it: "Here lies the curiosity of Clara R., who wanted to know too much." The smaller one, almost invisible: "Here lies the first breath of whoever will read these words and is about to leave." You are reading this inscription. You are about to leave.

scelte.testo: Go towards the sound of the door opening
Look for Ettore Morvell before going

pagine.testo: You look for Ettore. He's no longer on his step. He's not in the room—or at least, not in a form you can see. "Ettore?" you call. A sound—almost an answer—comes from one of the larger gears. The gear isn't moving, but it has a new, fresh inscription that wasn't there before: "I'm fine. Go." Then, just below: "Thank you." It's impossible—the metal of the gears should take tools and time to etch. But the inscription is there, deep and precise. You keep the image in your mind—that word "thank you" etched into the dark metal—as you walk toward the sound of the open door. The caretaker was right. The villa remembers every step. But now it stands still, and your steps can carry you out.

scelte.testo: Follow the sound to the open door
Return to the guardian's crypt to greet her

pagine.testo: You return to the corridor. Keep your left hand on the left wall—out of habit, even though the corridor is now still and will never change. The corridor takes you back to the caretaker's crypt. She's still sitting at the table, with her cups and thermos. She watches you arrive without surprise. "Is it done?" "I think so. The gears have stopped." She nods. A long silence. Then: "Ettore?" "She wrote 'thank you' on a gear." Another pause. Then—it's the first time you've seen her do it—the caretaker actually smiles. Not the tired smile from before. A genuine smile. "Good." She stands up. "Then I can go too." And the moment he says it, something in the room changes—the light, the air, the quality of the silence. "The villa stopped, but someone had to remember it from within. Now that it's stopped, I no longer have that task." He disappears. Not theatrically—he's simply no longer there.

scelte.testo: Exit the crypt towards the open door
Stay a moment in the empty crypt

pagine.testo: You stay in the crypt for a moment. The objects on the shelves are still there—all those remnants of past lives. The sextant, the compass, the clock. You touch them one by one. No sound. The objects are silent now—they've lost the voice they had when the villa was in motion. You take the compass from the top shelf. Not for any practical reason—the villa is stopped, the road is straight now. You take it because a compass is an object for orientation, and outside the villa you might need it to remember how the normal world works. On the caretaker's table, among the cups, there's still a bit of the dark liquid from the thermos. You pour a finger into the last cup. You drink it. It tastes of herbs and burnt wood, as before. But now it tastes of something else, too—something hard to describe. It tastes of the end.

scelte.testo: Exit the crypt towards the open door
Explore the villa still before leaving

pagine.testo: You explore the still mansion. It's completely different—without its turning mechanism, without the changing corridors, without the doors appearing and disappearing, it's just... a mansion. Large, old, full of interesting rooms, but nothing more than a building. You return to the foyer. The grandfather clock has stopped. The crystal chandelier no longer swings. The six-finger print still marks the marble floor—but now it's just a mark, without the sinister thrill it once had. You open the red door. The hall of mirrors is silent. The reflections are normal—just you, from different angles, with the expression of someone who has slept little and lived too much. You open the black door. The corridor is simply a corridor. You return to the foyer. The two doors. The stopped clock. The note in your pocket that says, 'Choose carefully. The villa remembers every step. "The villa remembered. And now it has stopped.

scelte.testo: Go towards the open door you heard the sound of
Exit through one of the two original doors

pagine.testo: You try the red door. Beyond it is still the hall of mirrors—but at the end of the room, where before there was only the wall, there is now an opening. A door that wasn't there. In fact—it was probably always there, but covered by the villa's mechanism that kept it hidden. Beyond the opening, a light. Not the dim light of candles or the bright light of the well—a natural light. Sunlight. Your heart does something it hasn't done in hours: it warms. You walk toward the light through the hall of mirrors. Your reflections accompany you, all synchronized, all with the same expression—relief? hope? both?—on the thousand reflected faces. The door opens into a vestibule you've never seen before, and the vestibule opens into a final door. A large wooden door with a simple handle. Sunlight filters through the edges.

scelte.testo: Open the door and go out
Please stop with your hand on the handle before opening

pagine.testo: You pause with your hand on the doorknob. Your hand trembles slightly. Why do you hesitate? The answer is complicated. You've spent—how much? Hours? Days? Years of villa-time compressed into an impossible-to-measure time—in this place. You know it. Not in the sense that you know your home, but in the sense that you know something that tried to absorb you and failed. There's something strange about abandoning it. Then you think of Ettore Morvell—who hesitated, and then no longer hesitated because the villa hesitated for him. You think of the caretaker—who hesitated for so long she didn't remember to do so. You don't want to become either one or the other. You tighten the handle. You throw open the door.

scelte.testo: Leaving the villa

pagine.testo: Three counterclockwise. One clockwise. The key turns with a precise resistance—as if each click were designed, as if each degree of rotation opened something specific. On the third counterclockwise turn, you feel something loosen inside the column. On the final clockwise turn, a sharp click—like the click of a high-quality lock—and then silence. Not a gradual silence. An instantaneous silence. As if someone had flipped a switch. All the gears stop simultaneously. The vibration stops. The hum stops. The heat of the column diminishes beneath your palm. And then—from the back of the villa, through all the corridors and rooms and mechanisms now stopped—you hear a sound you didn't expect: a door. A door opening. Ettore Morvell on his step sighs—a long, deep sigh like that of someone who has held their breath for decades. "There it is," he says softly. "The exit.

scelte.testo: Follow the sound to the exit
Spend a moment with Hector before going

pagine.testo: The corridor you follow toward the sound takes you—strangely, directly, without any impossible turns—into the hall. The hall that is your starting point. The cold marble floor. The crystal chandelier. The grandfather clock. The clock is stopped. The pendulum still in mid-swing. But the two doors—red and black—are both open. Not wide open—open a few centimeters, as if someone had just pushed them from the other side and then stepped aside. From the red door comes a light that wasn't there before. From the black door comes a smell—fresh air, wet earth, outside. You are in the hall for the last time.

scelte.testo: Exit through the red door into the light
Stepping out the black door into the fresh air

pagine.testo: The black door takes you back to the long corridor—but this time you don't have to choose between the stone staircase and the wooden one. Both staircases are gone. Where they once were, there's now only one exit: a low window in the wall, the size of a small door, through which sunlight shines. The window wasn't there. Either it was always there, and the villa hid it. Or the villa was keeping it closed, and now, with the mechanism stopped, it's opened. You go through it—you have to bend down, put your legs in first, then the rest—and out you go. You're out. You're really out.

scelte.testo: Breathe the fresh air and look at the villa from the outside

pagine.testo: You walk through the library, searching for something useful. The books that tell your story are all still—the last written pages are the moment you turned the key. After that, blank. But other books—the ones that tell the stories of others—continue. You see the life of the sailor, the painter, the ordinary person. On the last pages of each is a freshly added line: "The villa has stopped. You can leave now." You're not sure what that means. You're not sure you understand much of what happened in this villa. But the library now seems like a normal place—tall, extraordinary, full of impossible books, but normal in the sense that it doesn't threaten you. You descend from the library and follow the sound of the exit.

scelte.testo: Follow the sound to the open door
Return to the lobby

pagine.testo: In the hall of mirrors, the mansion stops, the reflections are all synchronized. No delay, no smile that isn't yours. Only you, reflected from a thousand angles, tired and disheveled and alive. The sphere on the alabaster pedestal—the one you touched at the beginning, the one that sent you back—is now transparent. Inside, there's no longer the shadow it once had. There's a miniature scene—a scale model of the mansion, perfectly detailed, with all the corridors and rooms. You observe it. In a tiny room, you see a figurine—yourself. You walk through the miniature mansion toward the exit. The figurine moves as you move. The mansion is still watching you. Perhaps it always will. But now its gaze is neutral—observant, not entrapping. You exit the hall of mirrors toward the light.

scelte.testo: Go towards the open door
Return to the atrium

pagine.testo: The wooden staircase takes you back to the kitchen. The moka pot on the table has stopped smoking—the coffee is cold. The sheet of paper with the very thin handwriting is still there, but when you look at it now, the words have changed: instead of "The drawer that won't open," it now says "You've found the way. Go." The kitchen seems smaller without the villa's mechanism that swelled its symbolic importance. It's just a kitchen—large, well-equipped, with its own pantry and trapdoor. Through the window—which you didn't remember was there—you see a garden. A real garden, with grass and trees and afternoon sunlight. The window is closed but not locked. You open it. The air from outside comes in—cold, damp, with the smell of earth after the rain. You've forgotten that smell existed.

scelte.testo: Going out the kitchen window into the garden
Return to the hatch to check the tunnel

pagine.testo: You look for another way out in the cellar. In the back walls—beyond the wine cabinet with the blue bottle—there's another door. This one wasn't hidden, you just hadn't noticed it. It's made of iron, with a simple lock. You open the lock. The door opens onto a brick corridor—different from the kitchen tunnel, lower, narrower—which after a few meters leads up to a door at ground level. You push the door. It opens outward. You're in a backyard of the villa—gray, cobbled, with a dry well in the center and a gate on the side. The gate is open. Beyond the gate is the road.

scelte.testo: Go through the gate onto the road
Return to the villa before leaving for good

pagine.testo: You've been back to the hall too many times. You're starting to understand the mechanism—the mansion brings you back when you make mistakes, but it doesn't punish you. It starts over. It's its method, as the caretaker used to say. The time you entered the black corridor and chose the stairs, then the kitchen, then the tunnel, then the caretaker—that was the right path. Every other choice brought you back here, in different ways. Now you know where to go. Not because you have a map—even if you have a sheet of paper in your pocket—but because you have memory. And memory, in this mansion, is the most precious thing there is. Doors in front of you: one red, one black. You know which one.

scelte.testo: Open the black door

pagine.testo: You're wandering around the stalled villa, looking for things you haven't seen yet. You find rooms you haven't explored: a music room with a dusty piano, its notes playing by themselves as you pass by. A conservatory filled with plants—how are they still alive?—with leaves of impossible colors. A room completely empty except for a chair in the center and, on the ceiling, an extraordinary painting of the night sky—so detailed you can make out constellations. There's no exit from any of these rooms. But in each one there's something that feels important to remember. As if the villa, now that it can't hold you, is trying to give you something. Then you hear the sound again—the open door, the call to the outside—and you realize you can't put it off any longer.

scelte.testo: Follow the sound to the exit
Visit the music room again before you go

pagine.testo: You return to the music room. The piano plays by itself—not a complete melody, but a series of single notes, at irregular intervals, as if searching for a chord. You sit down at the instrument. You don't know how to play—or at least, you don't remember knowing how to play. You press a key. A note. Then another. Then another. The piano's notes complement yours, as if it were improvising around what you play. For a few minutes, you make music with an empty, still villa. Then you stop, and the piano stops with you. The last note resonates in the air for much longer than usual. On the piano stand is a sheet of music. At the top of the sheet, instead of the title of the piece, it says: "Good luck outside." Close the sheet of music carefully.

scelte.testo: Going towards the exit for good
Return to the hall one last time

pagine.testo: In the conservatory, the plants are silent but present. Some have leaves that glow faintly, an impossible green. One, in a large terracotta pot, has white flowers that smell of something you can't place—something sweet, distant, familiar. You stop in front of this plant. The scent of the white flowers carries something with it: a fragment of memory. A garden. A hand holding yours. A voice saying something—you can't hear the words, but the tone is warm. Someone outside the villa is waiting for you. Maybe. You don't know who it is—the villa has taken that memory—but you know someone is there. The scent of the white flowers follows you as you leave the conservatory and walk toward the open door.

scelte.testo: Follow the scent and sound to the exit
Take away one of the white flowers as a souvenir

pagine.testo: The sound of the open door guides you through the villa, still as Ariadne's thread. You no longer have to keep your hand on the wall—the corridors no longer change. You no longer have to choose between stairs or doors or passages—there is only one clear, clean, straight direction. You step through the hall one last time. The stopped clock greets you with its motionless pendulum. The marble floor reflects your footsteps. The six-toed footprint is gone—or perhaps it has always been there and now you no longer notice it. The sound of the open door comes from the black door—which is open, you see that now. Beyond the threshold, instead of the long blue corridor, there is a short corridor that ends in a few steps. And that corridor ends in light.

scelte.testo: Moving towards the light
Stop in the foyer and look at the villa one last time

pagine.testo: You walk down the final corridor. It's short—five, six steps—and ends in a double French window that opens to the outside. Through the glass you see a porch, and beyond the porch a gravel driveway, and beyond the driveway an open gate, and beyond the gate the world. You push the doors. They open without resistance. The porch is gray stone, with rusty iron chairs that must have been white decades ago. On the porch floor are dry leaves—real leaves, fallen from real trees, blown by real winds. You pick up a leaf. You look at it. It's just a leaf—dry, brittle, autumnal orange. But it's proof that there's an outside, that there's an autumn, that there's something beyond Villa Morvell. You hold the leaf in your hand as you walk down the porch steps toward the gravel driveway.

scelte.testo: Walking towards the open gate
Stop for a moment on the porch

pagine.testo: You stop on the porch. You look at the villa from the outside—or almost: you're still on the porch of the villa itself, but the point of view is already different. The facade is of ivory-colored stone, the windows large and symmetrical, the multiple chimneys on the roof. It's a beautiful villa, in a severe way. From the outside, you can't tell anything about what's inside. Then you notice something: in one of the windows on the upper floor—the third from the left, the one mentioned in the book with the blank pages—there's a light. Not the electric light of the wall sconces, not the flame of the candles. A soft, almost lunar light, pulsing slowly. Then the light goes out. And the window is dark like all the others. You don't know what it was. Perhaps the villa saying goodbye. Perhaps the caretaker finding her way. Perhaps Hector letting go of something he's kept for decades. You go down the steps.

scelte.testo: Go towards the open gate

pagine.testo: The gravel driveway crunches under your footsteps—an ordinary, reassuring sound. To the right and left of the driveway are large trees—lindens, perhaps, with bare branches and dark bark. Dry leaves everywhere. You walk toward the gate. You don't have to run—the gate is open, the villa is still, there's no mechanism that can bring you back. You walk slowly, looking around, trying to register everything. Then you stop. You hear something. A voice—far away, from outside the gate, from across the road. A voice calling your name. A voice you recognize, even if you can't put a face to it. Someone is looking for you. Someone knew where you were, or hoped to find you. Your heart feels that warm thing again.

scelte.testo: Answer the voice and run to the gate
Approach slowly, with caution

pagine.testo: You walk around the perimeter of the villa, outside, in the garden. You look for other entrances or exits—not to go back inside, but out of curiosity. To understand what you just left. The villa is as large as it appears from the inside—perhaps larger. It has wings you haven't explored, windows at levels you haven't reached. It's an impossible structure—from the inside it seemed to contain more space than it physically could, but from the outside you realize it's simply a very large building. At the back is the gray courtyard with the dry well—the one from the cellar corridor. And on the other side of the courtyard, the gate already open onto a side road. Two exits. The front gate, the back road. Both lead outside. Choose the front gate—that's the one you hear the voice toward.

scelte.testo: Go to the main gate

pagine.testo: You try to go back inside—not because you want to stay, but because you forgot something in the hallway. A small object, perhaps. Or maybe it's just the reflex of the habit of returning, which the villa has forced upon you. But the door from the porch no longer opens. It's not locked—the handle gives—but the door doesn't move. As if something from inside is holding it. Not forcefully, simply still, like a weight. You knock. No response. Then, from the crack under the door, a piece of paper comes out. It's the piece of paper you found in the foyer at the beginning: "Choose carefully. The villa remembers every step." On the back, someone has freshly added: "You've chosen well. Don't come back." Put the piece of paper back in your pocket. You're not coming back.

scelte.testo: Go towards the open gate

pagine.testo: The road beyond the gate is a country road—paved but narrow, lined with tall trees. There's no traffic. The air smells of wet earth, dry leaves, something fresh, approaching winter. You walk along the edge of the road, not knowing where it leads. It doesn't matter—it leads out of the villa, and that's enough. After a bend, the road widens. Then there's a crossroads. Then, in the distance, the lights of a village. You stop and look back, the way you came. You can't see the villa—it's too far away, or hidden by the trees. Then you realize you're not sure how long you've been walking. It doesn't matter. You keep going.

scelte.testo: Continue towards the lights of the village
Stop for a moment in the middle of the road

pagine.testo: You stop in the middle of the empty street. You take a mental inventory of what you've experienced. The list is long and strange and full of elements you don't know how to catalog. You have in your pocket: the piece of paper from the hall, the golden key (again, for some reason), the compass taken from the crypt, a dry leaf from the portico. In your head: the face of Ettore Morvell looking at you with that hidden hope. The caretaker's final smile. The words engraved on the mechanism. The scent of white flowers in the conservatory. You don't have: your memories from before. You gave those—one to the caretaker, one to the villa, some you lost along the way. Do you know where you live? You're not sure. Do you know who's waiting for you? You heard a voice, but now it's silent. What you know is that you're outside. And that outside, things start again, one way or another. Start walking again.

scelte.testo: Walking towards the town lights

pagine.testo: Along the road you find a bench. There shouldn't be a bench in the middle of a country road, but there it is. You sit on it. A piece of paper sticks out from the side of the bench, tucked into the metal of the frame. You pick it up. It's a page torn from a book—one of the library books. Your book. It describes this moment—you sitting on the bench, with the paper in your hand, reading about yourself sitting on the bench with the paper in your hand. At the bottom of the page, after the description of the present, there's an extra line: "He'll get up. He'll walk toward the lights. He'll find something he doesn't expect, and it's exactly what he needs." You don't know what it is. But the library never lied—it told the story as it happened, without altering it. You get up. You walk toward the lights.

scelte.testo: Going towards the lights of the village

pagine.testo: The voice you heard calls again. Louder now—closer. It comes from beyond the trees, from the direction of the village. A voice you know the way you know a melody you've heard a thousand times—you don't know the title or the author, but you know every note. You start running. The gravel crunches, the leaves flutter, the cold air enters your lungs. You run without thinking of the villa, without thinking of the stopped gears, without thinking of Ettore or the caretaker or the keys or the mechanisms. You simply run toward the voice. Then you see it—beyond the trees, at the edge of the village—a figure standing at the edge of the road watching you come. You can't see it well from this distance. But the voice is its.

scelte.testo: Run towards the figure
Stop and walk the last few meters

pagine.testo: The exit is near. You feel it—not just the sound of the door, but something more physical: a change in the air, a different pressure, the way the light behaves. The villa is coming to an end. In your last steps inside—if it's still inside—you gather your thoughts the way you gather things scattered on a table before leaving. The key in your pocket. The piece of paper. The compass. The leaf. And the memories of the villa, which no one can take away from you because you lived them, not dreamed them: the perfect coffee in the kitchen, the caretaker's smile, the thank you engraved on the metal, the sound of the gears stopping. A short life inside a large villa. And yet—and this surprises you—it hasn't been all terror and confusion. There have been moments of something you can't call anything other than beauty. The final door is before you.

scelte.testo: Open the door and go out

pagine.testo: The gate is open. You walk through it. The iron gate is cold under your fingers—real, physical, unmistakably outside the villa. From the outside of the gate, you look back: the gravel driveway, the porch, the ivory-colored facade of Villa Morvell. The windows are all dark. The chimneys are smokeless. Then—and it takes you a second to realize what you're seeing—from the third-floor window, the third from the left, a light comes on. A soft, almost lunar light. You wave, even though you don't know who's there. The light pulses once—like a heartbeat—and then goes out. You turn.
