Ahead of you is the road, the trees, the distant lights of the village, and—at the beginning of a side driveway—a figure standing watching you approach. The voice you heard says your name.

scelte.testo: Go towards the figure
Stop and stare at Villa Morvell one last moment

pagine.testo: You stop and stare at Villa Morvell. From the outside, in the autumn afternoon, with the light slanting through the bare lime trees, it is simply beautiful. Imposing, serious, one of those villas built to last centuries. You don't want it to burn. You don't want it to collapse. You want it to remain there—empty, still, silent—for centuries to come. Because now that the machinery has stopped, it is just a building. A building with an extraordinary and complicated history, but a building. Maybe someone will buy it. Maybe it will become a hotel or a museum or an artists' residence. Maybe it will remain there, closed, waiting. The figure at the head of the driveway calls your name again. You turn and go.

scelte.testo: Go towards the figure

pagine.testo: Along the driveway are other objects—left there, who knows when, who knows by whom. A closed umbrella. An out-of-season straw hat. A book—bound in leather, with no title on the spine, just like the ones in the library. You open it. It's empty. Not blank—empty, the pages unprinted or written on, simply unused paper. A book waiting to be written. You take it. You don't know why. Perhaps because outside the villa you need something to tell, and an empty book is the right place to start. The driveway ends at the road. The figure is there, waiting for you, and as you approach you begin to make out its features.

scelte.testo: Come closer to the figure

pagine.testo: The world outside the mansion is quiet. Not silent—there are sounds, the birds, the wind in the trees, the distant whir of an engine—but quiet, in the sense that the sounds are in the right proportions. Not the overwhelming whir of gears, not the absolute post-mechanical silence. The normal scale of the world. You're walking toward the figure when you notice something you hadn't noticed before: the golden key in your pocket has grown warm. Not the heat of the body that warmed it—a heat of its own, as if it were generating energy from within. You take it out and look at it. The engraved snakes seem to move—it's just the light, you tell yourself, but you're not sure. Then the key cools. It becomes normal. It's just a piece of metal with engravings. You put it back in your pocket. Maybe one day you'll understand what it was. Or maybe not, and that's okay.

scelte.testo: Continue to figure

pagine.testo: The road between the gate and the figure is longer than it seemed. You walk for minutes—or perhaps it's tiredness that dilates time—and the figure remains the same distance away. Then you understand: it hasn't moved. It's there, still, waiting for you to arrive. It doesn't come toward you—it lets you take all the steps. As if it knows you need it. Every step on the gravel, on the asphalt, through the dry leaves, is a step into the normal world. Every breath of cold air is a breath of something that doesn't smell of wax and metal and mystery. You are re-entering the world. And the world hasn't changed. It's you who have changed—in ways you won't be able to measure for a long time, perhaps ever. The figure is close now. Close enough to see its eyes.

scelte.testo: Look into the figure's eyes and walk towards her

pagine.testo: You look at the villa one last time. Truly the last time. Then you ask yourself: if someone else enters, after you, will they find the villa still standing still? Or will the mechanism restart, like a heart after a shock, starting to beat on its own? You don't know. You can't know. You could even go back to check — but the villa has already told you, through the door that wouldn't open, not to come back. Trust the villa, in this. It has shown that it knows what it is doing. The gate is behind you. The road is ahead. The figure is there, patient, with that warm voice that says your name as if it will never stop.

scelte.testo: Reach the figure on the road

pagine.testo: One of the last objects you notice, on the edge of the road near the gate, is a watch. Not a wristwatch, not a pocket watch—a table clock, the kind with the bell alarm, abandoned there on the edge of the asphalt as if someone had forgotten it while getting out of a car. You pick it up. It's not the grandfather clock from the hall—that one's left inside, stopped on its mechanism. This is a normal clock, battery-powered, that tells the correct time—or at least, it tells an hour, and you have no way of knowing if it's correct because you don't remember how long it's been since you entered the villa. You hold it in your hand for a second. Then you put it back on the edge of the road where you found it. You don't need to know the time. Not yet.

scelte.testo: Go towards the figure

pagine.testo: You're a few steps away from the figure. Its features are clear now—a real person, wearing a heavy coat and hands in its pockets, watching you approach with an expression that mixes relief and something that could be anger and something that is definitely love. You don't remember its name. Not yet—the memories returned from the villa come in bits and pieces, like puzzles slowly reassembling themselves. But you recognize that face in the profound way that important things are recognized, without going through the brain. "It took you," the figure says. Not "where were you," not "are you okay"—just that, said in the voice of someone who has waited much longer than expected and has already decided not to make a drama out of it. "I know," you reply. "I'm sorry." "It doesn't matter." A hand comes out of the pocket and takes yours. Warm. Real. "Let's go."

scelte.testo: Go

pagine.testo: The figure six paces in front of you smiles at you. A full smile—not the janitor's tired smile, not Jeeves' painted smile—a real smile of someone who is happy to see you and doesn't try to hide it. Two more paces. One. Their hands find each other. Fingers intertwined. A squeeze—not hard, not desperate, but firm. The kind of squeeze that says, 'I'm here.' Then that voice, close by: 'You've been missing for three days. Three days. I've looked everywhere.' Three days. The house seemed both less and more. 'I found something,' you say. You still don't know how to explain the rest. Maybe there's no need. Maybe it's enough that you're here, outside, with someone's hand in yours. 'What?' You think about it. 'The way out.' The figure laughs—softly, in its throat. 'Of course.' A pause. "Shall we go home?

scelte.testo: Yes. Let's go home.

pagine.testo: As you walk together along the road to the village, you talk. Or at least, you try. The words to describe Villa Morvell don't exist yet—you'll have to invent them, or find them in the empty book in your pocket, or simply stop trying to describe it and let it become something else: memory, not story. The figure next to you listens without interrupting. Every now and then he nods. He doesn't ask for proof, he doesn't question, he doesn't suggest alternative explanations. He listens. This—he understands now—is exactly what you needed after the villa. Not explanations. Not questions. Someone who listens. The village is nearby. Its lights are warm in the falling evening. There are lit windows, sounds of normal life, the smell of someone cooking. Your stomach remembers that it exists.

scelte.testo: Enter the town with the figure next to it

pagine.testo: At the entrance to the village is a small square. A fountain in the center—this time a normal fountain, with water falling and simply making the sound of water. Benches. A large tree with almost all its leaves fallen. You sit there for a moment. The figure sits next to you. Shared silence—completely different from the silences of the villa. This silence is full of ordinary things: wind, fountain, distant footsteps, someone's dog barking once and then stopping. You take the golden key out of your pocket and look at it. Then you drop it into the fountain's basin. Not for any particular reason. Or perhaps for a very specific reason: the key belongs to the villa, and it's right that the distance between you and the villa should grow. The key touches the bottom of the basin and remains still. Not shiny, not mysterious. Just a piece of metal at the bottom of a fountain, like so many other objects forgotten in so many other places.

scelte.testo: Get up and move on

pagine.testo: You're almost done. You feel it—not as a deadline, but as the natural end of a story that has had a tortuous path but has always gone in this direction, towards this moment, towards this final step. The figure next to you still holds your hand. The lights of the village ahead. The smell of cooking in the air. You still have the villa in your mind—the stopped gears, Hector and his thanks, the caretaker who disappears when she no longer has a task, Jeeves with his key-offering. You will carry them with you. Not as scars, or at least not just as scars. As something closer to testimony. You've been in. You've come out. You stopped something that needed to be stopped. One more step.

scelte.testo: Take the final step

pagine.testo: Get out. Or you're already there—the boundary between inside and outside was less defined than you thought, and perhaps you cross it gradually over minutes or hours, not in a single instant. But at a certain point you are unmistakably outside. The air of the real world in your lungs. The ground of the real world under your feet. The sky of the real world above your head—autumnal gray, with a few patches of blue, and the low sun casting long shadows between the trees. Villa Morvell is behind you. You don't look at it anymore. In front of you is someone who knows you and was waiting for you. Beside you is a road. In your pocket you have a compass, a piece of paper, an empty book, and a dry leaf—the things you carry out of an impossible place, when you can't carry the memories of before. But the memories return. Slowly, as you walk. Like objects emerging from murky water: a name, an address, a day of Week, a habit. Your world, piece by piece, falling back into place. The villa has taken something from you. The villa has given you something. It's not a draw—there's no way to measure these transactions—but it is what it is. You walk. The village has warm lights in the windows and the smell of cooking in the air and the banal, wonderful sounds of people going about their normal lives. You're there.
